Notes: Blue supergiants — the hottest, most massive main-sequence stars. Lifespans of only 1–10 million years. Intense ionizing radiation sterilizes surrounding space.
Large, bright, blue main-sequence stars. Rare but highly visible. Short lifespans (10–100 Myr) and intense UV leave little time for life to develop.
White/bluish-white main-sequence stars. Fast-rotating with lifespans of ~1–2 billion years. High UV output limits habitability despite wide habitable zones.
Yellow-white dwarves with wide habitable zones. Emit significant UV but good candidates for life-bearing worlds.
Yellow dwarves fusing hydrogen for ~10 billion years. Optimal conditions for life development.
Orange dwarves, stable for up to 30 billion years. Longer window for life to evolve than G-type stars.
Red dwarves — the most common stars in the universe. Extremely long lifespan but planets face tidal locking, intense stellar flares, and minimal UV.
Moderate-mass stars in late stellar evolution. High luminosity pushes habitable zone very far out.
Substellar objects lacking mass for hydrogen fusion. Extremely dim and cool (~500–1400K). Habitable zones are negligibly small if they exist at all.
Burnt-out cores of low/medium-mass stars. Earth-sized but extremely dense. Narrow habitable zones exist but are unstable.
Massive evolved stars violently shedding their outer layers via intense stellar winds. Extremely hot and luminous. Rich in heavy elements.
Magnetized neutron stars emitting precise radiation pulses. Useful as timekeepers.
Special Effects: Shield Nullification; can build Quantum Catapult

Notes: Collapsed massive stars with gravity so strong even light cannot escape.
Special Effects: -50% Disengagement Chance; +50% Emergency FTL Cooldown; can build Black Hole Observatory and Matter Decompressor

Notes: Ultra-dense supernova remnants. Only ~10km across but more massive than a G-type star.
Special Effects: -50% Sublight Speed; can build Quantum Catapult
